title: Chapter 17: The Art of Being a Target
paragraphs: The road to Agdash, the ancient stronghold of the High Elves, was paved with white stone that was now hidden under a thick layer of gray, lifeless ash. Trees along the roadsides had long since turned into withered black skeletons, stretching gnarled branches to the leaden sky. In this world, birds did not sing. The silence was broken only by the steady crunch of the footsteps of the four travelers.

They had been walking for several days already. Kirah stepped with agonizing caution, trying to set his colossal, claw-strewn paws in such a way as not to raise dust. The demon was panicky about nearing the city. His crimson veins pulsed dimly in rhythm with his anxiously beating heart.
Dzhanavar, on the contrary, strode broadly, having thrown his giant axe over his shoulder.

"Do you know what they say in my forest about long roads?" the werewolf yawned, his fangs glinting. "A wolf does not ask how far it is to the goal. A wolf simply makes it so that the goal itself begins to fear him. Augh. Though, to be honest, my paws are humming."
Rene walked slightly behind, gracefully stepping over clumps of ash. The smell of her minty cigarette whimsically mixed with the smell of rotting ozone.

"We are walking to certain death, my dear green philosopher," the fox said melancholically, addressing Yashil's back. "A city of elves. With a demon, a werewolf, and a goblin. This isn't even audacity. This is aesthetic suicide. They will shoot us from the fortress walls simply out of a sense of beauty."

"If we do not come to them, they will just die thinking that only darkness remains beyond the walls," Yashil replied quietly, without turning around. "Someone must show them that a light can be lit in the darkness."

Soon the outlines of Agdash emerged from the mist. It was a monumental, overwhelming sight. Colossal walls of pale jade and basalt, etched with protective runes, reached high into the skies. But this greatness was sickly: the runes barely flickered, and deep cracks covered the marble, like wrinkles on the face of a dying elder.
The gate guards noticed them from a hundred paces away.

A sharp, guttural cry rang out in the air. The melody of an elven horn, once beautiful, now sounded like a hoarse cry for help. A wall of shields grew instantly on the walls, and the tips of heavy crossbow bolts glinted predatorily in the narrow arrow-slits.

The captain of the guard, a tall elf in tarnished gold plate, stepped out onto a narrow balcony above the locked gates. His face was pale, sharpened by years of malnutrition and sleepless nights. His eyes glittered feverishly. He looked at the approaching procession, and his face twisted with crystal-clear, undiluted disgust.
"Stop, scum!" the elf's voice, amplified by ancient magic, hit the ears like a whip. "One more step toward the Gates of Life, and you will be made into pincushions for arrows!"
Kirah whimpered softly, instinctively tucking his horned head into his shoulders. He tried to step back behind Dzhanavar's back to seem smaller.

"Take it easy, Pointy-ears," Dzhanavar growled, gripping his axe with both paws. The fur on his scruff stood on end. "Don't wake a sleeping beast. We came in peace, but my axe is always ready for a constructive dialogue."

"Shut your maw, hound!" the captain spat, clutching his sword hilt so hard his knuckles turned white. His gaze darted from one outcast to another. "I see before me an offspring of the Abyss, a filthy half-wolf, a port wench of the beast-kin, and..." the captain faltered, staring at Yashil standing in front. His voice broke into a poisonous hiss: "And a goblin. Higher powers, even a rotting reality has not birthed such a vile joke! What irony... filth leads monsters."
Rene gracefully flicked her ash, throwing a long, significant look at Yashil that read a cold: "I told you so."
Yashil did not stop. He took another even, calm step forward.

"We seek no quarrel, Captain," the goblin's voice was quiet, but it strangely carried over the dead wasteland, reaching the ears of every guard on the wall. "We seek shelter. And we offer our hands to protect these walls."
The captain of the guard let out a short, barking laugh, full of madness and pain.
"Protection? From a goblin?!" The elf abruptly turned to his soldiers and gave a sign with his hand. "Open the small wicket. I will show this scum his place myself."

The heavy iron door opened with a clang. The captain, accompanied by two guardsmen, stepped outside the walls. He paced out, heading straight for Yashil. Dzhanavar growled, Kirah tensed, ready to release his dark mania, but the goblin raised a small green hand, ordering them not to intervene.
The elf approached closely. He was three times Yashil's height. Casting off a plate gauntlet, the captain struck the goblin across the face with all his might.

The sound of the blow echoed off the marble walls. The little goblin could not keep his feet and fell into the gray, radioactive ash. Thick, dark-green blood splashed from a cut cheekbone, instantly soaking into the dry earth.

"You are nothing!" screaming, the elf kicked the prone Yashil in the chest. "Your race has been stealing from us for centuries! You are parasites! And now you come here and dare offer us help?! My brother died yesterday in the maw of such a demon as the one standing behind your back!"
The air suddenly became heavy, like lead.

Kirah let out a sound that made blood run cold—a low, rumbling groan of a tectonic fault. The crimson veins on the demon's body flared with a blinding light. The giant stepped forward with a roar, his claws turning into black sabers. The guards on the wall raised their crossbows in panic.
But before the demon or the wolf could make a dash, Yashil with difficulty raised himself on his elbows.
"No, Kirah. Stand down," Yashil's voice trembled with pain, but in it sounded such an overriding strength that the thousand-ton demon froze as if struck by lightning.
The goblin slowly, breathing heavily, rose from the ground. He did not wipe the blood pouring down his chin. Yashil stepped forward and again stood face to face with the heavily breathing captain.

He did not reach for a weapon. He did not start shouting curses or pleading for mercy. He looked the elf straight in the eyes. In the goblin's gaze was neither the rage of the rejected nor the humiliation of a slave. There splashed the bottomless, quiet sadness of a being who sees another's pain more clearly than its own.

"Your brother was killed by the Void, Captain," Yashil said softly. "But the Void has no face. The skies that tore your life apart cannot be struck with a sword. But I am here. I am right here. I am ugly, I am weak, and I am very easy to hate."
The elf's hands, clutching the sword, trembled finely.

"If my blood," Yashil took another step forward, baring his unprotected chest under the very point of the elven blade, "is capable of muffling your pain... if my death will patch the hole in your heart—kill me right now. I will not resist. And my friends will not touch you. I promise."
The silence hanging over the gates of Agdash became deafening.

The captain looked at the little goblin, and his familiar, understandable world was falling to pieces. All his centuries-old pride, all the hatred nursed by years of a hopeless war, shattered into fragments against the wall of this impossible, absurd compassion. The elf expected aggression. He expected cowardice. But he was not ready for a creature he considered filth to show a nobility surpassing the teachings of the High Elves.
The sword in the captain's hands faltered and slowly, inexorably lowered its point into the ash.
The elf let out a stifled, pitiful sound, like a sob. The great warrior of the ancient race stepped back a pace, unable to bear the weight of his own grief and the suddenly surging, burning shame.
"You... you are not a monster," the captain whispered, not taking his widened eyes from the green blood on Yashil's face. "What are you?"
"I am just a traveler who is tired of watching us kill each other before the end of the world does," Yashil replied quietly.

Dzhanavar slowly lowered his axe, blinking his amber eyes in surprise. Rene, whose face for a moment lost the mask of eternal cynicism, quietly exhaled a stream of minty smoke. "I swear by the spirits of my ancestors..." the fox whispered. "This idiot really knows how to win without baring a blade."
The captain of the guard was silent for a long time, his head hung low. Then he turned to the wall and raised his hand. "Lower the crossbows!" he commanded hoarsely but masterfully.

With the clang of mechanisms, the lethal weapons were put away. The captain turned to Yashil again, this time avoiding looking him straight in the eyes—it was too hard to bear that wise, all-forgiving gaze.

"I cannot let you into the city. Such is the law of the Elders, and I have no power to break it," the elf said in a completely different, tired tone. "But you may make camp in the shadow of our buttresses. You will not be touched."

He turned and went back to the small gate wicket. Но at the very threshold the elf stopped, took a clean linen flask with water from his belt and, without turning around, threw it into the ash next to Yashil.
A small drop of kindness in a sea of hatred. But for Yashil, this drop meant that the ice that had bound this world had begun to melt.
